summary: Gruber analyzes the competitive landscape for Windows 7's launch, arguing that Microsoft's real competition isn't Mac OS X but rather the massive installed base of Windows XP users who may be indifferent to upgrading. He suggests that the hundreds of millions of XP holdouts may not have avoided Vista because it was bad, but because they simply don't care about software upgrades at all. He highlights the orthogonal nature of Apple-Microsoft competition: Microsoft sells OS licenses mostly for low-end hardware, while Apple sells premium hardware for profit. Gruber concludes that Windows 7 could be good for both companies simultaneously, with Microsoft recapturing the commodity market and Apple continuing to grow in the premium segment.
key_points: Windows 7's primary competitor is Windows XP (running on ~80% of PCs), not Mac OS X, making market share comparisons between Mac and Windows misleading
The massive XP holdout may reflect user apathy toward computing rather than dissatisfaction with Vista — people who don't care about computers won't upgrade regardless of quality

Apple and Microsoft compete orthogonally: Microsoft sells OS licenses for mostly low-end hardware, Apple sells premium hardware and cares about profit share (25%+ of U.S. computer profits) not market share
Phil Schiller publicly argued Apple would benefit from Windows 7 because millions of XP users heading to stores to upgrade would encounter Macs as an alternative
Windows 7 could succeed for Microsoft while simultaneously being good for Apple, as each dominates a different segment of the market
money_quotes: What if the reason why most PCs are still running XP has nothing to do with whether Vista is 'good' or 'bad', but rather is the result of indifference on the part of whoever owns these untold millions of XP machines?
Perhaps Windows 7's competition isn't so much XP as it is apathy.

The idea that Windows 7's quality will spur upgrades from XP is predicated on the fact that the people holding out on XP make their computing choices based on quality. But if that's the case, why exactly are they still running Windows XP?

I think it's hard to overstate the fact that, with the explosion of the Internet as a universal communication medium, hundreds of millions of PCs have been purchased around the world by people who don't care about computers or software at all.
The truth is that no one really knows why Vista fared so poorly in the market. It defies a simple explanation.
It's entirely possible that Windows 7 will be good for both Microsoft and Apple.
This gets to the heart of the weird, orthogonal, indirect competition between Windows and Mac OS X.
key_insight: Windows 7's greatest obstacle isn't a competing OS but mass user apathy — hundreds of millions of PC owners who don't care about software quality and won't upgrade regardless, making Microsoft's real battle one against indifference rather than Apple.
